images.caption: Figure 1:CoDA-Benchassesses an agent’s capacity to leverage code to solve complex problems in data-intensive environments.

Figure 2:Construction method ofCoDA-Bench. We construct semantically coherent environments using dataset co-occurrence graphs, extract tasks with closed-form answers from real Kaggle notebooks, and verify quality through adversarial evaluation.

Figure 3:Dataset co-occurrence network showing 21,122 Kaggle datasets (nodes) and their co-usage relationships (edges). Node size
indicates usage frequency; colors represent communities detected by the Leiden algorithm.
Figure 5:Results under giving oracle data or discovering in community.

Figure 6:Results over various interaction rounds of agents.
Figure 7:Error attribution of agents across failed cases.

header: Appendix BGraph Construction and Community Detection

images.caption: Figure 8:Network of 31 selected communities used inCoDA-Bench. Node colors indicate community membership, node sizes reflect notebook usage frequency, and edge widths represent co-occurrence strength. The network exhibits clear clustering patterns corresponding to different data science domains.

Figure 9:Detailed visualization of community_0 (Classic ML Benchmarks). The network shows 130 connected datasets (after removing 24 isolated nodes). Node sizes represent notebook usage frequency. Labels indicate the 15 most central datasets by degree. Key datasets include Iris Species, Pima Indians Diabetes, Credit Card Fraud Detection, Water Quality, and Medical Insurance.

Figure 9:Detailed visualization of community_0 (Classic ML Benchmarks). The network shows 130 connected datasets (after removing 24 isolated nodes). Node sizes represent notebook usage frequency. Labels indicate the 15 most central datasets by degree. Key datasets include Iris Species, Pima Indians Diabetes, Credit Card Fraud Detection, Water Quality, and Medical Insurance.

Figure 10:Detailed visualization of community_2 (COVID-19 & Global Geography). The network shows 70 connected datasets (after removing 18 isolated nodes). Labels highlight the 15 most central datasets. The community reflects the integration of pandemic data with country-level statistics for comparative analysis.
